content: When you first enable LTO on your Cargo build, the most immediate surprise is often not a performance gain but a massive spike in compile time that eats up your CI pipeline. This happens because Rust's optimizer needs to materialize all intermediate representations before it can perform cross-crate inlining, which requires reading every crate definition into memory and generating a single, monolithic IR blob for the final link step. The fundamental distinction between thin LTO and fat LTO lies entirely in how this massive blob is constructed and distributed at runtime. Thin LTO keeps the optimization information embedded directly within each compiled object file alongside its own code, meaning your binary size grows slightly but you retain full backward compatibility with older toolchains; every crate can be linked independently while still benefiting from cross-crate inlining decisions made by the optimizer. Fat LTO, conversely, writes a separate metadata file containing all the optimization data and requires an explicit linking step to merge this metadata back into your binaries before they are ever executable on disk, which gives you maximum optimization potential but demands careful artifact management during your build process. The critical edge case that trips up experienced developers is when you combine PGO with LTO without understanding how their interaction affects the compiler's inlining decisions and symbol visibility. When you run a PGO profile collection pass using crates like cargo-pgo, the compiler learns which functions are called frequently enough to warrant inlining across crate boundaries; however, if you then enable fat LTO before the optimization passes complete, the optimizer may prematurely finalize its cross-crate inlining choices based on default assumptions rather than your actual usage patterns, causing it to inline functions that should remain separate or conversely fail to inline functions that are hot paths. The solution is always to run PGO profile collection first using cargo pgo collect, then analyze the resulting profiling data with a tool like `cargo pgo print`, and finally enable LTO in your final build configuration so the optimizer can make informed decisions about which functions to inline across crate boundaries based on real-world usage statistics rather than static analysis alone. You will notice that Rust's standard library types like Arc, Mutex, and Rc are inherently compatible with these optimizations because their internal representation remains stable regardless of whether you use thin or fat LTO, but when working with custom traits or generic functions defined in your own crates, the visibility rules become critical; specifically, items marked as #[inline] will only get cross-crate inlined if they are reachable from a function that has already been inlined by another crate in the same build tree. The build time cost of enabling LTO is roughly proportional to the number of crates you include and their total line count, so for large projects with hundreds of dependencies it can increase compilation times by several minutes per run; however, once compiled, the runtime overhead of thin LTO binaries is negligible compared to fat LTO which may introduce additional metadata parsing steps during execution. A common gotcha involves symbol visibility where you might see errors like undefined references even after enabling LTO because the optimizer has decided not to inline a particular function due to its complex signature or dependencies on external crates, so you must ensure that all functions intended for cross-crate inlining are properly annotated with #[inline] and do not rely on unstable features or crate-specific abstractions that prevent the compiler from generating valid IR across boundaries. If your project uses cargo-audit or similar security tools alongside LTO, remember that some static analysis passes may fail to detect issues within optimized code because they cannot resolve symbols after cross-crate inlining has occurred, so you should run these checks before enabling LTO rather than expecting them to work correctly afterward.
